coredata.dc:title: A multioutput DC–DC converter for solid-state transformer-based fast electric vehicle charging stations with batteries of different ratings
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Sunny, Amal C.
Muttaqi, Kashem M.
Sutanto, Danny
coredata.dc:description: The fast-growing demand for electric vehicles (EVs) necessitates the fast-charging DC infrastructure for EV batteries. The Solid State Transformer (SST)-based power electronic solutions are promising for fast charging applications as they have better controllability and power density than the grid frequency transformer-based solutions. This paper proposes a multioutput DC–DC converter for SST-based fast EV charging stations, which can charge three types of EVs with batteries of different ratings. The three EV battery voltages are 800V, 400V, and 48V, considering different categories of EVs (heavy, medium, and light-duty) such as buses, trucks, cars, and two and three-wheelers. Compared to the existing solutions, the proposed converter uses less number of semiconductor switches while providing isolation among all ports. The reduced count of semiconductor switches also lowers the demand for the gate driver circuits needed to drive the switches. The detailed analysis and operation of the converter, along with the control, is presented in the paper. The performance of the proposed converter is substantiated through detailed simulation analysis and experimental validation using a scaled-down laboratory prototype. The outcome of the simulation and experimental study proves the proposed converter’s effectiveness for DC fast-charging applications.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: CAUL institutions 2023: Hybrid journals full funding
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electric Vehicle (EV)
Fast charging station
Multioutput converter
Power converter
Solid-state transformer (SST)

head.ce:title: A multioutput DC–DC converter for solid-state transformer-based fast electric vehicle charging stations with batteries of different ratings

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Australian Power and Energy Research Institute (APERI), School of Electrical, Computer and Telecommunications Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Information Sciences, University of Wollongong, New South Wales, 2522, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Australian Power and Energy Research Institute (APERI), School of Electrical, Computer and Telecommunications Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Information Sciences, University of Wollongong

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Australian Power and Energy Research Institute (APERI), School of Electrical, Computer and Telecommunications Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Information Sciences, University of Wollongong, New South Wales, 2522, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fast-growing demand for electric vehicles (EVs) necessitates the fast-charging DC infrastructure for EV batteries. The Solid State Transformer (SST)-based power electronic solutions are promising for fast charging applications as they have better controllability and power density than the grid frequency transformer-based solutions. This paper proposes a multioutput DC–DC converter for SST-based fast EV charging stations, which can charge three types of EVs with batteries of different ratings. The three EV battery voltages are 800V, 400V, and 48V, considering different categories of EVs (heavy, medium, and light-duty) such as buses, trucks, cars, and two and three-wheelers. Compared to the existing solutions, the proposed converter uses less number of semiconductor switches while providing isolation among all ports. The reduced count of semiconductor switches also lowers the demand for the gate driver circuits needed to drive the switches. The detailed analysis and operation of the converter, along with the control, is presented in the paper. The performance of the proposed converter is substantiated through detailed simulation analysis and experimental validation using a scaled-down laboratory prototype. The outcome of the simulation and experimental study proves the proposed converter’s effectiveness for DC fast-charging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The DC power supply plays a pivotal role in modern power systems owing to the rapid growth of Electric Vehicles (EVs), which have gained significant attention as they have advantages such as noiseless operation and eco-friendliness [1,2]. Further, the development of battery technologies facilitating longer driving ranges and less charging times have contributed to the widespread acceptance of EVs. Even though the EVs have several advantages, their widespread acceptance is limited due to the lack of fast charging options and the range issues. Therefore, the development of DC fast charging stations, along with the advancement of battery technologies, is essential to the EV market [3,4].

The onboard charger that can be powered from a single-phase residential AC supply is a popular solution for charging EV batteries, and this method of charging has been categorized into Level 1 and Level 2 [5,6]. The Level 1 and Level 2 AC charging uses 120 V and 240 V supplies, respectively. However, this type of AC charging solution, which takes longer hours to charge the EV batteries fully, is not a prominent solution when considering long-distance traveling. One possible solution is the CHAdeMO fast charging standard for charging EVs with DC voltages up to 1 kV at high power [7]. The charger needs to be located off-board and connected to the MVAC (medium-voltage AC) grid, considering the high power rating. The capacity and the number of EVs to be charged simultaneously are two crucial factors of a fast charging station. The battery voltage levels of electric cars are between 350 V–400 V [8], and for heavy-duty EVs (trucks, buses, etc.), the same is around 800 V, considering the battery’s higher capacity [7]. Moreover, for two and three-wheelers, the battery voltage is 48 V [9]. Therefore, a fast-charging station must be capable of catering EV batteries with three different DC voltages simultaneously.

The EV charging stations reported in [10,11] use grid frequency transformer-based solutions. The system in [10] deploys the three-phase diode rectifier and DC–DC buck converter for the AC–DC conversion and control after stepping down the MVAC voltage to LVAC using a grid frequency transformer. The EV port can be controlled using the duty cycle of the buck converter. However, deploying a diode rectifier in the first stage produces a distorted input current on the AC side. The scheme in [11] deploys an AC–DC controlled rectifier after stepping down the MVAC voltage, and it can improve the quality of the input current. However, the schemes reported in [10,11] cannot charge three different categories of EVs simultaneously, and the grid-frequency transformer is bulky and heavy.

A Solid-State Transformer (SST) is a power electronic device that can replace traditional grid frequency transformers. Unlike the traditional bulky, uncontrolled grid frequency transformer, the SST is compact and can control voltage, current, and frequency in a power system using power converters [12,13]. The SSTs are central to next-level fast charging stations as they are compact, controllable, and have excellent power quality compared to the grid-frequency transformer-based solutions [14,15]. The topology reported in [16] realizes the voltage conversion from MVDC (medium-voltage DC) to LVDC (low-voltage DC) using a DC–DC SST, where the topology has two stages, with the first stage a half-bridge module followed by a DAB (dual active bridge) converter, and the LVDC port can be used for charging EVs as shown in Fig. 1(a). The SST in [16] is modified as a single stage with reduced switch count in [17] as shown in Fig. 1(b). Since the SSTs reported in [16,17] have only one LVDC port, they cannot charge three different types of EVs. The SST reported in [18] uses a cascaded multi-level converter (MLC) in the first stage for the MVAC to MVDC (medium-voltage DC) conversion, followed by DAB converters for the MVDC to LVDC conversion. The high-frequency transformers provide isolation between low and high-voltage ports. The voltage levels MVAC, MVDC, and LVDC are 7.2 kV, 3.8 kV, and 400 V respectively. The LVDC port can be regulated by controlling the phase shift of the DAB converter, making it suitable for fast EV charging applications but not for charging three different categories of EVs as there is only a single LVDC port. The traction transformer reported in [19] uses a resonant DC–DC converter in the second stage instead of a DAB converter compared to the scheme in [18]. To maximize efficiency, the resonant converter is operated at its resonant frequency, and the converter operates in an open loop without controlling the LVDC port, and the system has only a single LVDC port for EV charging applications. The topologies reported in [20–22] can be used for the MVDC to LVDC voltage conversion. However, there is no provision for charging three different categories of EV batteries simultaneously. The multiport current source SST reported in [23] has two low-voltage ports for charging two categories of EV batteries. However, there is no isolation between these two ports. When compared to the other SSTs reported in the literature, the SST-based fast charging station presented in [24] can charge three different categories of EVs simultaneously. The SST-based solution in [24] uses a cascaded MLC and DAB for the MVAC to LVDC conversion, and it provides multiple output ports on the LVDC side for charging different categories of EVs. Moreover, all the ports are isolated using high-frequency transformers. However, the scheme reported in [24] has a higher number of semiconductor switches.

A schematic block diagram of the multi-output SST for charging three different categories of EV batteries is shown in Fig. 2, and the circuit realization of the same is given in Fig. 3. In Fig. 3(a), two DC–DC buck converters are paralleled to the LVDC port; Port 1, Port 2, and Port 3 are for the 800 V, 400 V, and 48 V batteries, respectively. However, the system is not isolated among different DC ports, and the voltage ratings are different. Moreover, the low voltage gain of buck converters does not allow a wide voltage range between all the EV ports [25]. Therefore, 800 V to 48 V conversion is not possible with the system shown in Fig. 3(a). This problem can be addressed by adopting the scheme in Fig. 3(b), where all the LVDC ports (Ports 1, 2, and 3) are isolated, and different EV battery voltage levels can be catered by selecting the turns ratio of the high-frequency transformer. However, this scheme requires a higher number of semiconductor switches. The higher number of semiconductor switches also escalates the requirement for gate driver circuits [26].

This paper proposes a novel multioutput DC–DC converter for SST-based fast EV charging stations for simultaneously charging three different categories of EVs. The proposed converter has the advantages of the least semiconductor device count and isolation compared to the existing solutions. The rest of the paper is arranged as follows: Section 2 presents the operation and control of the proposed converter, followed by simulation results in Section 3. The experimental results are given in Section 4, and Section 5 concludes the paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Operation and control of the proposed multioutput converter
ce:section.ce:para:
The schematic of the proposed multioutput DC–DC converter is shown in Fig. 4. In Fig. 4,
V
2
,
V
3
, and
V
4
are the low voltage DC ports for charging three different categories of EV batteries. The ports
V
2
,
V
3
, and
V
4
are designed for 800 V, 400 V, and 48 V respectively. The proposed converter has galvanic isolation between the input port and each of the output ports using three high-frequency transformers integrated into the SST, as shown in Fig. 4. The proposed SST operates at a higher frequency (20 kHz), unlike the conventional EV charging solutions based on the bulky grid frequency transformer. Thus, the proposed converter offers a compact solution while providing galvanic isolation among different ports without using conventional grid-frequency transformers. The detailed switching strategy and the key waveforms of the converter are shown in Fig. 5. The switches in every half-bridge are operated in a complimentary manner, with a constant duty cycle of 0.5, and the phase shift (as shown in Fig. 5) is used to control different ports. Compared to the scheme reported in Fig. 3(a), all the ports are isolated from each other, and the number of semiconductor devices is significantly reduced compared to the scheme devised in Fig. 3(b).
The different variables used in the power converter are given in Table 1.

The expressions for power transfer between different ports can be derived as follows. From Fig. 5,

V

2

The capacitors
C
1
and
C
2
are required to carry higher rms currents as they are connected in series to the primary of the high-frequency transformers. However, as the voltages across the capacitors
v
c 1
and
v
c 2
are the internal variables of the converter, a higher peak-to-peak ripple voltage can be allowed across
C
1
and
C
2
, which reduces the capacitance values. Thus,
C
1
and
C
2
can be realized by using low-value film capacitors with higher rms current rating and negligible series resistance. A similar design approach for the converters reported in [27,28] is followed.
The voltage stress across various components of the proposed multioutput converter is given in Table 2.

The power balance of the proposed converter is given in Eq. (22).
(22)
V

For controlling the voltages
V
2
,
V
3
, and
V
4
, three voltage control loops are used, as shown in Fig. 6. Proportional–integral (PI) controllers process the error generated between the reference and feedback voltages, and the output of the PI controllers adjusts the phase-shifts
ϕ
1
,
ϕ
2
, and
ϕ
3
. As all the half bridges in the proposed converter are operated at a constant duty cycle of 0.5, the generated phase shifts from the control loops shift the carriers used to generate the switching pulses of all H-bridges. The dynamic equations of the proposed converter are as follows:

Eq. (23) can be linearized by perturbing the variables in the following form.

From Eq. (39), the value of
a
1
can be calculated by substituting the parameters. Substituting

1
into Eq. (36) and using Eq. (28), the value of

can be determined. Finally, Eq. (31) can be used to calculate

. The transfer functions

( s )
The controllers (PI-2 and PI-3) can be designed by using the same approach used to design the controller PI-1 as given in Eqs. (28)–(39).

The use of individual voltage control loops for each port facilitates simultaneous regulation of all three output voltages, even under dynamic loading conditions. Furthermore, since each port is controlled independently using PI controllers, power sharing and battery charging are effectively managed regardless of load variations on the other ports.

The performance of the devised multioutput converter is substantiated through a detailed simulation analysis using the MATLAB/ Simulink platform. The MVDC voltage (
V
1
) is set as 3600 V, and the EV battery voltages are taken as
V
2
= 800 V,
V
3
= 400 V, and
V
4
= 48 V. The converter is switched at 20 kHz, and the phase shifts
ϕ
1
,
ϕ
2
, and
ϕ
3
are used as control variables to control different battery ports.

The simulation results of the converter with no loads connected at the EV charging ports are shown in Fig. 7. The voltages are regulated at the desired reference values with
V
2
,
V
3
, and
V
4
as 800 V, 400 V, and 48 V, respectively. The capacitor voltages
v
c 1
, and
v
c 2
are 400 V each as per Eq. (21). The voltages
V
1
and
v
c 2
are shown as 0.5* the actual voltages in Fig. 7. It is evident from Fig. 7 that DC voltage regulation is maintained even at no load conditions.

Simulation results of the converter under different load conditions are given in Fig. 8. In Fig. 8(a), the EV port
V
2
is loaded at 350 kW, and the battery is charged at 800 V. In Fig. 8(b), the EV ports
V
3
and
V
4
are loaded at 75 kW and 10 kW with DC voltages of 400 V and 48 V, respectively. In Fig. 8(c), the EV ports
V
2
and
V
4
are loaded at 350 kW and 10 kW with DC voltages of 800 V and 48 V respectively. The performance of the converter under dynamic load changes is given in Fig. 8(d), where the EV ports
V
2
,
V
3
, and
V
4
are loaded at 350 kW, 75 kW, and 10 kW, respectively, up to the time at t = 0.4 s. The 350 kW load from the port
V
2
is disconnected at t = 0.4 s; therefore, the current
I
2
comes to zero. At t = 0.8 s, the 350 kW load is connected back to port
V
2
. Further, at t = 1.2 s, the 75 kW load from port
V
3
is disconnected and again connected at t = 1.6 s. Similarly, the 10 kW load from port
V
4
is disconnected at t = 2 s and is connected back at t = 2.4 s. The transient response of the converter during start-up and dynamic load changes are given in Fig. 8(e) and (f) respectively. It is evident from Fig. 8(d) that voltages at each EV port are regulated at the desired reference voltages irrespective of the load changes in other ports. Further, the transient response of the system proves the converter operation without exceeding the safety limits during starting and dynamic load changes. From Fig. 8, it is clear that the proposed converter can simultaneously charge multiple EV batteries of different voltage ratings.

A scaled-down prototype is built in the laboratory to validate the performance of the proposed multioutput converter. The various parameters used in the prototype are given in Table 3, and the photograph of the prototype is shown in Fig. 9. The input DC voltage (
V
1
) is obtained by using a single-phase Variac and a diode rectifier (model: SKD 51/14, make: Semikron). As the actual EV batteries of the same voltage ratings are expensive, resistive loads are connected to respective EV ports (
V
2
,
V
3
, and
V
4
) to demonstrate the CV (constant voltage) charging of the EV batteries. A four-channel Digital Storage Oscilloscope (model: DSOX4024A, make: Keysight) is used to observe the experimental waveforms of the proposed converter from the prototype. A high bandwidth current probe (model: N2781A, make: Agilent, 150A/10MHz) is used for measuring the current.

The outcome of the experimental study is presented in Figs. 10 and 11. The gate–source voltages of various switches are given in Fig. 10, where the converter is switched at 20 kHz, with a period of
50
μ s
. The switching pulses of top and bottom switches are complimentary in every half-bridge of the converter with dead time and are operated at a duty ratio of 0.5, as shown in Fig. 10. The gate–source voltages are phase-shifted to create the phase shift between different H-bridges, as shown in Fig. 10. It is to be noted that the switching pulses of
S
1
and
S
3
are the same, and similarly, the switching pulses of
S
2
and
S
4
are the same, as is the case with the other H-bridges.

Experimental results of the converter under different load conditions are given in Fig. 11. Fig. 11(a) gives the voltage waveforms under no load conditions. The four channels in the oscilloscope (Ch1, Ch2, Ch3, and Ch4) are used to measure the voltages
V
1
,
V
2
,
V
3
, and
V
4
. The
V
1
,
V
2
,
V
3
, and
V
4
voltages are 300 V, 150 V, 100 V, and 48 V respectively. In Fig. 11(b), the EV port
V
2
is loaded at 144 W (
V
2
= 150 V,
I
2
= 0.96). In Fig. 11(c), the EV port
V
3
is loaded at 114 W (
V
3
= 100 V,
I
3
= 1.14 A), and in Fig. 11(d), the EV port
V
4
is loaded at 40.8 W (
V
4
= 48 V,
I
4
= 0.85 A). From Fig. 11, it is clear that a devised multioutput converter can simultaneously cater three different EV batteries of different voltage and power ratings. Moreover, the EV voltages at all three ports are regulated at the desired reference values irrespective of the load on the other ports.

A comparison of the proposed converter with state-of-the-art SST-based schemes for the DC fast charging applications is shown in Table 4. The topologies reported in [16–22] have only a single LVDC port, which can be used for DC fast charging and, therefore, to maintain uniformity for every additional EV port, a DAB converter (8 switches) is paralleled to the LVDC port as shown in Fig. 3(b). All the solutions in Table 4 provide isolation among different ports. Moreover, the maximum voltage stress on the MVDC and LVDC switches are the same. Further, all the benchmark solutions reported in Table 4 and the proposed topology use high-frequency transformers for isolation and have almost the same levels of control complexity. It is evident from Table 4 that the proposed converter has the least semiconductor switch count when compared to all other schemes reported in the literature. Moreover, the lower requirement of semiconductor switches reduces the cost of the driver circuits apart from the price of the switches. Table 4 proves that the converter devised in this paper is superior as it has a lower switch count while maintaining similar maximum switch voltage stress and isolation features compared to the existing solutions.

The proposed converter is scalable for large-scale EV charging stations capable of charging three different categories of EV batteries. The three high-frequency transformers integrated into the SST provide galvanic isolation among different EV ports, ensuring safety at high-power levels. Additionally, each output port of the SST can be independently controlled under different loading conditions, allowing flexible power management based on the specific demand of different EV batteries. The advantage of a reduced number of semiconductor switches also becomes significant when scaling the system for higher-power applications, as it lowers the overall cost of the semiconductor switches and the associated gate driver circuitry. This makes the proposed SST both technically and economically a promising option for larger capacity EV stations.

While building a high-power prototype of the proposed multioutput SST, the associated thermal implications can be addressed through a detailed loss analysis of the converter. Suitable heat sinks can be selected based on the computed loss profile so that each semiconductor device operates within its safe thermal limits. Additionally, forced-air cooling can be deployed to improve heat dissipation during continuous high-power operation of the converter. Furthermore, suitable semiconductor switches can be selected by calculating the RMS current and voltage stress, thereby avoiding over-rating the switches. All these design approaches can enhance the reliability of the system under continuous high-power operation, even with less number of switches. To enhance the reliability of the converter further, different protection techniques can be adopted in both hardware and control. The short-circuit and over-voltage protection can be achieved by sensing the current and voltage at different ports using sensors. The microcontroller can withdraw the switching pulses or shut the system when an over-current or over-voltage condition is detected. Additionally, the short circuit of half-bridge module can be avoided by adding suitable dead time between the top and bottom gate signals of every half-bridge. Thermal protection can be obtained by using temperature sensors or thermistors. Additionally, fault flags can be added to the system for protection and fast diagnostics of the fault.

For practical implementation of the proposed converter, a proper design strategy can be adopted to suppress the electromagnetic interference (EMI) arising from the converter’s high-frequency switching operation. In the PCB layout, the parasitic loop inductance can be minimized by decreasing the distance between different switching nodes and the return paths. Additionally, decoupling capacitors can be placed closer to the switching devices to reduce the parasitic inductance. The three high-frequency transformers can be designed by optimizing the winding geometry to achieve the desired leakage inductance for efficient power transfer while limiting parasitic capacitance to reduce high-frequency noise. Further, the single-ended switching pulses from the micro-controller can be converted into differential signals before being fed to the gate drivers, thereby reducing the signal noise. Additionally, passive filters can also be employed in the converter design to mitigate EMI.

While the proposed multioutput SST offers the advantage of a reduced semiconductor switch count, the high-frequency transformer and the switches (
S
1
–
S
8
) connected to the MVDC port (
V
1
) must be rated to handle higher currents at high-power levels. This is because the power required by the loads connected to the ports
V
3
and
V
4
is also processed through these components. Moreover, all the topologies listed in Table 4 have this limitation, as the ports
V
3
and
V
4
are obtained from the port
V
2
using additional DAB converters, as shown in Fig. 3(b).

This paper presented a novel multioutput DC–DC converter for SST-based DC fast charging stations for EVs. The proposed converter can charge three different categories of EVs simultaneously. The battery voltages are 800 V, 400 V, and 48 V, considering different categories of EVs such as buses, trucks, cars, and two and three-wheelers. One of the main advantages of the proposed converter is the reduced count of semiconductor switches, which not only reduces the cost of the switches but also reduces the requirement of the driver circuits needed to drive those semiconductor switches. Further, being an SST-based solution, the proposed converter isolates all the ports without using the traditional grid-frequency transformer. It thus offers a compact solution with less size and weight compared to grid-frequency transformer-based charging solutions. The performance of the system has been substantiated using simulation analysis and experimental studies using a scaled-down laboratory prototype. The results proved the effective load regulation and dynamic performance under different loading conditions. The load regulation is maintained effectively on each port irrespective of the load conditions on other ports, which proves the practicability of the proposed topology as, in reality, the multiple EV ports will have different loading conditions based on the requirements of different EV batteries. Additionally, the results proved the converter can simultaneously charge three types of EV batteries of different ratings. Further, the proposed converter is compared with the other solutions reported in the literature regarding the key features such as the number of EV ports, switches, isolation, and maximum voltage stress across the semiconductor devices. The thorough analysis, simulation, experimental study, and comparison with the existing solutions prove the effectiveness of the proposed multioutput converter.

Amal C. Sunny: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Kashem M. Muttaqi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation. Danny Sutanto: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Australian Research Council (ARC) Discovery Project , DP200103405.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Sha, X. Wang, and D. Chen, “High-efficiency current-fed dual active bridge DC–DC converter with ZVS achievement throughout full range of load using optimized switching patterns,” IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics, vol. 33, no. 2, pp. 1347–1357, 2018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 7.2 kV three-port SiC single-stage current-source solid-state transformer with 90 kV lightning protection
IEEE Trans. Power Electron.

Divan, “7.2 kV three-port SiC single-stage current-source solid-state transformer with 90 kV lightning protection,” IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics, vol. 37, no. 10, pp. 12080–12094, 2022.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: A.C. Nair, B.G. Fernandes, A Solid State Transformer based Fast Charging Station for all Categories of Electric Vehicles, in: IECON 2018 - 44th Annual Conference of the IEEE Industrial Electronics Society, 2018, pp. 1989–1994.

IEEE Trans. Power Electron.

IET Power Electron.

IEEE J. Emerg. Sel. Top. Power Electron.

IEEE Trans. Ind. Electron.
